data.title: 10 Highest Rated Dividend Kings For Generations Of Income
data.description:
It’s always exciting to watch an investment shoot up triple digits in a year. In 2025, those were mostly AI and tech stocks, which I write about a lot here on Seeking Alpha. They’ve also been in the spotlight for the last few years as technology continues to develop.

The thing is, not everyone is built for the volatility that comes with these high-risk, high-reward investments. Plus, there’s something to be said about the peace that comes with consistent, reliable cash flow through dividend investing.

That said, “peace” isn’t always a sure thing with dividend stocks either. Some offer high distributions, only for it to become painfully obvious that they can’t sustain their own payments- at least once you have a look under the hood. Some look like a decent option, but then you see a history of inconsistency and unreliability when you dive deeper.

That’s why setting stringent, non-negotiable criteria when choosing your dividend stock makes sense. Sure, it’s not always going to be easy, especially if you’re starting out. So let me make it quick and simple for you by laying out my top 10 Dividend Kings that, barring something really big, will pay a lifetime of income, and then some, while also explaining why they’re in the mix.

What are Dividend Kings?

Dividend Kings are companies that have increased their dividend payouts for 50 years or more. That’s impressive all by itself, but when you look deeper into the “50” mark, you’ll realize that it would take a lot of discipline, resilience, and adaptability for a company to reach that goal. I’m talking about tackling multiple economic cycles, seismic technology shifts, and ever-changing consumer demands, all while growing their income and cash flow to cover dividend growth.

As of January 29, 2026, there are 56 Dividend Kings. So let’s see which ones are the best.

Selection criteria

When narrowing down any investments I might want to own for years, I like to start by considering several factors. For this analysis, I used Seeking Alpha’s Portfolio feature to create and customize my Dividend Kings list and to start the analysis.

Now, I’m not new to due diligence. I’ve been swimming through news and company filings for over two decades to form an opinion on potential investments. But when a platform gives you access to a comprehensive, objective grading system that takes every relevant metric into account, you might be tempted to take advantage of that convenience, which I am doing today.

Now, for newer investors, dividend yield is often “enough” to warrant a buy. I’m telling you now, though, that it shouldn’t be the end-all, be-all for your choice. It’s also important to evaluate if the yield is good enough to justify the risks you’re taking and meaningfully contribute to your income goals, and that usually varies from person to person.

I also considered the company’s payout ratio. For those unfamiliar, the dividend payout ratio is the percentage of a company’s earnings (or free cash flow) that it pays out as dividends. Higher numbers suggest the company may struggle to increase the dividend (at least meaningfully). My hard cap here is 70%. Anything above is removed from the list.

Then, there’s dividend safety. It considers earnings, free cash flow, operating cash flow, balance sheet strength, industry norms, and the overall stability of all of the above. I also considered dividend growth in the list. Seeking Alpha conveniently grades dividend stocks’ growth and safety. I limited the results to companies rated C- and above.

Then, I also checked the Quant, SA, and Wall Street ratings for each stock. It never hurts for your dividend stock choice to have a stronger consensus by the broader market, though some of them may have Hold ratings due to short-term headwinds or muted growth expectations.

This is particularly evident with the Quant rating, which is a proprietary rating system that takes all relevant company data, distills it into five factors: value, growth, profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions, then assigns an overall score based on all five. And, of course, short-term movement can pull down the scores. But remember, these investments are for the long run.

On that note, the last couple of criteria are profitability and valuation grade. The premise is simple: the better the company grows its profits, the better positioned it is to increase its dividends. And in general, Dividend Kings are high-quality picks, but even great businesses can be poor investments if bought at overstretched valuations.

Why are some performance metrics excluded?

You may notice that percentage price performance, short-term returns, and other commonly cited market metrics are not discussed here. My omission here is intentional. I’m less interested in short-term performance, as we often invest for the long run. Short-term price action tends to add more noise than any meaningful insight. A stock can post strong 1-, 3-, or 5-year returns while quietly becoming overvalued or stretching its payout capacity.

Arranging the list

I then arranged the results from highest to lowest valuation grade, because, at the end of the day, dividend investors want to buy companies that are not only reliable dividend payers but also attractively priced. Then I removed any entries with blank values to get the final list.

Top 10 Dividend Kings: The final list

So, with all that out of the way, here’s the top ten list of Dividend Kings to own for generations of future income.

ABM Industries

First up is ABM Industries (ABM), a facilities management company that provides janitorial, engineering, parking, and other building services to commercial, industrial, and institutional clients. The company has 57 years of consecutive dividend increases under its belt, plus a B- rating for both dividend safety and growth.

However, it does have a 2.75 (Hold) Quant rating, though I feel it more than makes up for its good SA and Wall Street scores with its highest valuation grade (A-) on the list.

Its dividend yield is around 2.5%, and its low 31% payout ratio practically assures further increases.

Now, the stock price has been trending down in the last year (a contributor to that valuation grade), though we may be seeing a reversal with its most recent moves.

Northwest Natural Holding Company

Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN) is a regulated natural gas utility that provides energy and water services primarily in the Pacific Northwest. The company boasts a 70-year streak of dividend increases, though its 69% payout ratio makes me just a little wary of its ability to grow the dividend in the future. Speaking of which, the company’s dividend growth is also slightly underwhelming, given its utilities sector, though the 4% forward yield can be quite attractive to some investors.

The company also has a solid B+ valuation grade, backed by lower-than-sector-average ratios (mostly) across the board.

Stanley Black & Decker, Inc.

Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) is one of the more recognizable names on this list- and one of my own favorite tool brands. The company manufactures and markets power tools and engineered products used for industrial, construction, and consumer markets.

At a ~4% forward yield, an A- dividend safety score, a B+ valuation, and 58 years of straight increases, SWK is a popular choice among dividend investors. However, its 69% payout ratio is bumping up against my personal hard cap, and its less-than-stellar profitability grade makes me a little nervous about potential profit speed bumps along the way.

Target Corporation

Next up is Target Corporation (TGT), one of the world's largest retailers. The company has increased dividends for the 54th consecutive year. Now, I like Target personally. It has solid dividend ratings across the board, and its 60% payout ratio is just below my usual safety threshold. Plus, the company pays over 4% yields, so what’s not to like?

Well, if I were nitpicking, I’d say the Quant and SA hold ratings might scare off some investors, but, again, we’re in this for the long run, and its current price weakness (down ~28% in the last year) may present a good entry opportunity.

Black Hills Corporation

Black Hills Corporation (BKH) is another regulated electric and natural gas utility, this time providing energy services to customers across several Midwestern and Western U.S. states.

The company’s Wall Street ratings are solid buys, though its Quant rating lags at a Hold. Meanwhile, its dividend ratings are quite decent, except for a C- on growth.

Still, the company has a 54-year streak of dividend growth and yields of 3.9%, above the sector median of 3.34%.

AbbVie Inc.

At the halfway point is AbbVie (ABBV), the biopharma offshoot of Abbott Labs (ABT) - another notable Dividend King. AbbVie has performed well over the last 12 months (+25%) and maintains a solid A+ profitability grade. It also has a decent yield, though the 69% payout ratio is something to watch out for.

Still, the overall dividend scorecard remains solid, and its overall ratings aren’t bad, either.

Archer-Daniels-Midland Company

Next up, Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (ADM) produces ingredients, animal feed, and biofuels while sourcing, storing, and transporting crops worldwide.

Overall, ADM's dividend scorecard is decent, with only a C on growth, though its 55% payout ratio is low enough for me not to worry about its cadence of increases.

Becton, Dickinson and Company

Becton, Dickinson and Company (BDX), another notable entry, develops, manufactures, and sells medical devices, systems, and various chemicals and reagents. The company has increased its payouts for 54 years, and its overall dividend scorecard makes it quite an attractive choice.

However, do note that this is the first entry on the list with a C+ valuation grade.

Kimberly-Clark Corporation

Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB) is a global consumer staples company that manufactures personal care and tissue products under well-known brands like Huggies, Kleenex, Scott, and Kotex. This second-to-last entry is also second-to-last in performance (-23%) for the last year, though a nearly 5% forward yield still makes the price dip a potential entry opportunity.

However, I do need to point out that this is the only company on the list with a sweeping hold rating across all three review criteria.

PPG Industries, Inc.

Last, but not least, is PPG Industries (PPG), the coatings and specialty materials company that produces paints, coatings, and finishes for industrial, automotive, aerospace, and consumer markets. The company pays a ~2.5% yield (58% higher than the sector median) and maintains a decent 38% payout ratio.

Admittedly, PPG is quite a solid pick based on its other criteria, though the C+ valuation grade is ultimately why it’s in 10th place.

Notable Exclusions

Some readers might be wondering why more popular Dividend King picks are missing from this list. Let me lay them out here, plus the reasons why they were excluded.

  • Coca-Cola Company (KO): D- valuation grade.

  • Walmart (WMT): F valuations grade, low yield.

  • S&P Global (SPGI): F valuations grade, low yield.

  • The Procter & Gamble Company (PG): D valuations grade.

  • Altria (MO): High payout ratio.

A word of caution

I’m not gonna lie, I found Seeking Alpha’s portfolio feature surprisingly flexible and a great starting point for research. The Screener tool is even better when you’re into more granular searches.

But the keyword here is “starting point.” Even now, after selecting these companies based on objective ratings, I would still like to dive further into each and every one to get a firmer view of how they are as potential investments.

Nothing beats due diligence, and doing it yourself allows you to take the driver’s seat. But to actually drive, you need a car, complete with a motor and wheels. That’s what Seeking Alpha is, at the end of the day- a vehicle for research.

data.title: FDVV: Dividend Equity ETF, Strong Dividend Growth, Long-Term Income, And Momentum
data.description:
The Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV) is an index ETF focusing on U.S. equities, with smaller investments in international stocks. FDVV is closer to the S&P 500 than most dividend ETFs, which means greater tech exposure, more consistent and higher total returns in the past, but only a 2.8% dividend yield. FDVV's strong fundamentals make the fund a buy, and it should be of particular interest for investors desiring some measure of tech exposure, somewhat uncommon for dividend ETFs.

FDVV - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

FDVV tracks the Fidelity High Dividend Index. Said index invests in relevant U.S. and developed market equities subject to a basic set of inclusion criteria. Weights are a complicated matter, and take into consideration market-cap, sector size, and three relevant equity-specific factors:

Overall, FDVV's approach all but ensure a portfolio reasonably similar to that of the S&P 500, with a strong focus on U.S. mega-cap equities. Largest of these are as follows:

FDVV currently invests in 106 different companies, resulting in a well-diversified portfolio, albeit one more targeted than the S&P 500. Most of the equities in the fund are included in the S&P 500, but most of the S&P 500 isn't included in FDVV. The fund selects only some of the constituents of the index, hopefully the better ones.

As mentioned previously, FDVV's weighting scheme considers market-cap, but that is not its only factor. Average portfolio market-cap should be high, but below-average, as is indeed the case: FDVV's average market-cap stands at $206B compared to $438B for the S&P 500.

FDVV's below-average market-cap increases risks somewhat, as smaller companies are generally riskier, more volatile than average. FDVV is far from a small-cap ETF though, so I don't think the differences here are all that significant. Looking at the fund's largest holdings, it's companies like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), and Visa (V), all mega-cap stocks. The S&P 500 does lean even harder on these, especially when looking at the weights, but FDVV is still a large-cap ETF.

The fund's approach also considers sector size, which, surprisingly, does not end up with balanced sector exposures, or at least not as much as I expected. I'm seeing significant underweight positions in consumer discretionary, health care, communication services and industrials. On the flipside, utilities, energy, financials, and real estate, are overweight.

FDVV is moderately underweight tech, with most dividend ETFs being significantly underweight tech. Although this isn't necessarily a positive, it is definitely an important fact for investors to consider. Specifically, most income investors are significantly underweight tech, as tech companies rarely pay significant income. FDVV is one of the few with good tech exposure, and some amount of income (not much but still above-average). I know some income investors want to increase the tech exposure of their portfolios, and FDVV might help in that regard.

Finally, although FDVV is an index ETF, the index seems niche enough that moderate outperformance or underperformance is possible. Significant differences in performance between FDVV and the S&P 500 seem unlikely, as the fund seems too similar to the index across fundamentals, but smaller or moderate differences seem possible.

Dividend Analysis

FDVV's weights are somewhat dependent on several dividend metrics, which should result in above-average dividends. FDVV's 2.8% dividend yield is higher than that of the S&P 500, as expected. It is also higher than that of most dividend ETFs in the market, with some exceptions.

On a more negative note, it is obviously not a particularly high yield on an absolute basis, with most bonds and bond sub-asset classes yielding more. At the same time, I imagine a 2.8% yield is a deal-breaker to many income investors and retirees.

FDVV's dividend growth track-record is quite strong, with the fund seeing consistent dividend growth throughout the years, generally in the high single-digit range.

Although the starting yield isn't too great, the growth definitely is, which resulted in a respectable amount of income for long-term shareholders in the past. Specifically, the fund's 10y yield on cost stands at 6.6%, a good figure for an equity ETF. In contrast, the S&P 500 figure is 3.9%, quite a bit lower than FDVV. Figures are as follows:

Overall, FDVV provides investors with an above-average 2.8% dividend yield, a strong dividend growth track-record, and strong long-term income (in the past). These are all important benefits to shareholders and should be of particular interest to more long-term dividend growth income investors. For lots of investors, especially retirees, present income is more important, so the fund's low starting yield is probably a deal-breaker.

Valuation Analysis

FDVV focuses on higher-yield equities, which tend to sport cheaper valuations (because both are correlated or caused by low prices). Compare FDVV's valuation metrics:

With those of the S&P 500:

As can be seen above, FDVV trades at a significant discount to the S&P 500 across metrics. Said discount could lead to sizable capital gains moving forward, which brings me to my next point.

Performance Analysis

As FDVV is an equity ETF, a significant portion of its total returns should consist of capital gains. FDVV leans higher-yield equities, which tend to be older, more established companies with fewer growth prospects and CAPEX, and hence more cash to deliver to shareholders. As such, I would expect FDVV to see somewhat lower capital gains than the S&P 500. As the differences here are not all that significant, and as so many other factors impact equity returns / price gains, this expectation of mine isn't particularly strong, but I do believe this is the likelier scenario.

FDVV's price gains have been significantly below those of the S&P 500 since inception. Due to their magnitude, results were worse than expected, by me at least. Below-average price gains led to underperformance too, as these were much greater than the increase in income.

Results above were due to, or coincided with, significant growth and tech outperformance, and hence underperformance of ETFs underweight growth and tech. FDVV is less underweight these sectors than its dividend equity ETF peers, but still underweight relative to the S&P 500.

On a more positive note, recent performance is much stronger, as sentiment has much improved for these securities and sectors. I'm seeing slight outperformance these past five years:

And since my last article in early 2024:

Overall, and in my opinion, FDVV's performance track-record is average enough to not significantly alter the fund's investment thesis (or lack thereof, if that were the case). Specifically, FDVV's above-average dividends and its cheap valuations form its investment thesis, and the fund's performance track-record does not meaningfully detract or add to this.

Conclusion

FDVV is a broad-based equity ETF focusing on higher-yield U.S. equities. FDVV's above-average dividends and cheap valuation make the fund a buy. As the fund's starting 2.8% dividend yield is quite low on an absolute basis, the fund should be of particular interest to more long-term dividend growth investors.

data.title: The Economics Of The AI Bubble
data.description:
Recently, I wrote an article covering what some CEOs had to say at Davos. Discussions at the World Economic Forum are always interesting because they tend to be thematically focused on economics but also focused on the speaker's domain.

In Satya Nadella's case, one of the CEOs I covered in that article, the focus of his talk was on artificial intelligence. That's natural as the CEO of Microsoft (MSFT). We would expect him to have AI on the mind right now.

A week later, I'm still thinking about Nadella's talk. I've been ruminating on the fact that he never once said the words "data center."

Instead, he opted for the very colloquially clunky "token factories." The longer I've thought about it, the less I think it's about optics. My first explanation for why he would use this term was because he was somehow worried about actually saying data centers. But the more I've thought about it, the more I've come to believe that Nadella really does fundamentally see data centers as factories, in that they have a very industrial application to the economics of AI. The output is tokens, a very quantifiable metric for data center production.

How a company uses those tokens for their AI models, efficiently or inefficiently, is a different question. But being able to sell the tokens in the first place puts you in control of a commodity source, one that has very high barriers to entry (capital costs). This is how natural oligopolies form--if the hyperscalers weren't that already from being so dominant as software firms.

Tokens As A Commodity

Large language models ("LLMs") run on tokens, due to how they parse data. For reference, a typical one-sentence question to an LLM like OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT that gets a one-paragraph response may consume between 2,000-5,000 tokens.

In October 2025, Google (GOOG) processed 1,300,000,000,000,000 tokens.

What's interesting to think about through this lens is that the tokens themselves are not representative of future economic output but of the potential for future economic output.

Some of these tokens will go to queries that result in no economic activity--what that number is, I don't know. I suspect that a significant amount of the tokens being processed by current LLMs are being used for very low to no economic end and that most of the generation we do is for "slop," or AI-generated content deceivingly passed off as human-made content to some end, usually ads.

Others of these tokens will go to queries that directly benefit economic activity and create excess productivity by allowing businesses to leverage generation to assist in their workflows. How that's going is still unclear with mixed signals coming from studies being done. I am increasingly finding myself on the side of AI helping productivity but replacing some jobs already. You can see more of my discussion on this in a recent article I wrote, a mea culpa of sorts on AI productivity.

The cost of these tokens is dropping quickly when including free token usage, which is likely the least economically productive use; we are seeing costs halve in the last year.

At the same time, token usage growth from giants like Google is growing quickly. That 1.3Qa they hit in October was up significantly from the 480T tokens they processed in May of last year. The rate at which users are demanding tokens slowed a bit toward the end of the year but is still on a tremendously fast growth trajectory.

GPUs, the machines that work in the factories to make tokens, are also commodities themselves in a sense, since there is little competition among GPU manufacturers (hello, NVIDIA (NVDA)); we can track their costs through rental rates. These rates have been falling as well, although not as much as token costs. GPUs introduce depreciation to the equation with hyperscalers, as they are a consistent cost from upgrades to newer models every few years, or maybe even sooner for cutting-edge labs.

The other side of the equation with GPUs is how efficient models are with their use. GPU power can be translated directly into tokens, which then can be translated into LLM output.

When benchmarked and tested, we can not only track how good LLMs are at doing intelligent tasks but also how much it costs to achieve that.

Note that in this example, the ARC-AGI-2 test costs to complete the test are rising, although not linearly with increases in score. The models are getting more efficient, a good sign.

If models get more efficient, then we should expect a greater economic potential for each token; output and productivity could grow even without an exponential increase in GPUs or token output itself--although we are definitely getting the latter through all the hyperscaler data center build-out plans.

Those build-out plans are increasingly becoming more and more relevant to economic growth in the U.S. and are now driving a significant amount of GDP growth.

The optimist take, and the one that I think Nadella would have here, is that capital expenditures are heavy now because the token factories need to be built, and models are expensive for their outputs now because they haven't been trained enough yet, but those are both processes that will play out.

Once they have, perhaps in a few years, we will have not only an abundance of token factories and therefore tokens, but also models that can turn those tokens into very efficient economic output. The energy spent on token factories could then imply tremendous future economic output.

Economic growth being tied to energy output, measured in tokens consumed, would be advantageous for the U.S. as a net energy exporter. Our electric grid will absolutely need some work, but that will also bring growth from the infrastructure spending needed.

The "AI Bubble" Risk

I'm on record as saying that I used to think we were in a clear bubble, but I don't think that way anymore. Namely, I've been deferring to an argument I made back in November, in "Are We Valuing the Markets Incorrectly?" where I discuss how profit-adjusted valuations make the S&P 500 buyable even at today's prices.

And I get it--my opponents only need to do one thing in response (and they do, trust me): post this image.

The relationships between large and small tech companies in AI are bubble-shaped when we draw them that way, but also in how many of the deals are reciprocal in some fashion. This, unfortunately, is just how modern corporate relationships tend to work. In comparative advantage economics, it's better to make relationships between different firms and pass the same money around, so long as each firm in the chain is adding real value, than to try and in-house everything you need.

My response is that there is actual value being moved around, and all the GPUs being deployed in most of these deals (note how NVDA is in the center of the bubble drawing) are actually being put to use. This is vastly different from past bubbles like Dot Com because much of the circular deals being done were to lay infrastructure that generated no economic output; it was called "dark fiber." The supply side was built, but there was no demand to soak it up, so the fiber optics were never lit.

Tokens, the ultimate output of GPUs and of the deals being made between tech firms, are being used. There are no dark GPUs. This is an absolutely critical part of the equation. Bubbles are inherently supply-side problems. But there is demand, and demand isn't slowing down as AI diffuses through the economy and more and more people integrate it into their workflows.

On top of that, cloud revenues are up. Cloud services are one of the ways in which AI is deployed at scale, and so an inflection up is a real positive for the demand of AI tools.

They are using it in their workflows more and more; over a quarter of workers report using AI at work at least once a week, with 12% reporting daily use. How productive these endeavors are is a different question, and I expect to see a slow ramp in productivity as integration goes less seamlessly than the AI optimists forecast.

Of course, we can also see that tech and finance inordinately benefit from AI tools, and so we should expect the gains being had from AI integrations to not be diffused evenly between industries.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I think that Satya Nadella was onto something when we tried to make "token factories" work over "data centers." I won't be adopting the switch in verbiage personally, but I believe that it is telling of how Nadella views the economics of AI.

Increasingly, we are able to track and model token consumption as to how much economic potential it has, and there is good reason to believe that not only are we going to have a surplus of data centers creating tokens for LLMs to use for creating economic output. Productivity gains are to be had, and looking at tokens as a commodity and Microsoft (among other hyperscalers) as the gatekeepers to those commodities helps one understand the argument around why AI may not be in a bubble.

Risks still remain in the market, as it is driven by these firms, which are priced as if they have already executed well on this rollout. Missteps could cause markets to falter from these all-time highs, but I don't believe that it is a bubble that will pop suddenly. The economics are just not there to support that, from my view.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: LendingClub: Originations Strength At A Modest P/E
data.description:
The 2026 market is looking to be an incredibly tricky one to invest in, and the Q4 earnings season is likely to make volatility spike even further. Amid a choppy market and the potential for waning enthusiasm for large-cap tech stocks, I continue to emphasize investing in small and mid-cap stocks that offer much better value.

LendingClub (LC), in my view, is a stock well worth owning. The consumer loan originator is showcasing accelerating new loan growth, while expanding net interest margins are defying downward interest rate pressures. The company is now minting true EPS, despite very low P/E multiples. The stock also just fell nearly 10% after reporting strong Q4 results and issuing a robust 2026 outlook, denting part of the rally over the past year. It's a great time, in my view, to open a new position in this stock on the dip.

I last wrote a buy article on LendingClub in October, when the stock was trading at $18 per share. Since then, LendingClub has continued to rally steadily upward, though I think the company's recent acceleration in origination trends, and its excellent credit metrics, continue to justify the stock's rally. I reiterate my buy rating here.

The first and foremost point that investors should be wary of here is that LendingClub offers tremendous growth at a very reasonable price, especially after its robust FY26 outlook. The company is guiding to 21-31% y/y growth in originations, which represents quite a healthy pace over an already-strong year in FY25. It's also estimating 42-55% y/y growth in pro forma EPS. We will note here that part of the underlying >40% earnings growth is due to the company electing a different accounting treatment for loan originations, effectively recognizing more of the expected loan yield as revenue upfront. Still, we look at the underlying originations growth midpoint of 26% y/y.

We note that at current share prices near $20, LendingClub trades at just an 11.4x P/E ratio. That's a bargain that is truly tough to find elsewhere in the market, especially for a company whose underlying organic growth metrics are trending well north of >20% y/y. Stay long here and use the recent, temporary dip in LendingClub as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through LendingClub's latest quarterly results in greater detail. For LendingClub, the entire business runs primarily on the growth in its originations: and here, we're pleased to see trends accelerating. Originations of $2.60 billion in the quarter grew 40% y/y, which accelerated three points over 37% y/y growth in Q3.

We note here that the company has enjoyed a diverse set of upside catalysts that has enabled strong originations growth. It has leveraged automation in order to analyze more favorable pricing for its loans, making LendingClub loans more attractive for borrowers. Also, the company has seen improving credit performance. This, in turn, has made LendingClub loans easy to sell and unload off the company's balance sheet to institutional investors, giving LendingClub access to easy monetization post-origination.

Per CEO Scott Sanborn's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Our substantial originations growth was driven by continued product innovation and marketing expansion, while also supported by improved marketplace pricing and sustained credit outperformance. Our discipline, combined with our advanced underwriting capabilities delivered 40% to 50% better credit performance versus our competitive set, and we're seeing stable performance and consistency in our borrowers' behavior.

Strong credit performance continues to support loan investor demand with marketplace revenue increasing 36% year-on-year, driven by higher marketplace volumes and loan sales pricing improving back towards our historical range."

Now let's dig into how originations translate into revenue. As shown below, LendingClub generated $266.5 million in revenue (+23% y/y) in the quarter, which handily beat Wall Street's expectations of $262.0 million (+21% y/y) by a two-point margin.

Income from originations is included in the company's non-interest revenue, which is just shy of 40% of the company's total. Non-interest income grew 38% y/y, closely in line with originations growth.

Meanwhile, like most banks, LendingClub earns the majority of its revenue from its invested balance sheet, most of which is allocated to its own loans. Yes, LendingClub is facing pressure from the Fed's interest rate cuts, which will overall reduce interest rate spreads. However, the catalyst that LendingClub has up its arsenal is the growing popularity of its checking and savings accounts. Here, the company is noting strong synergies with many new account openings coming from current loan borrowers, and many accountholders also more likely to originate a new loan. Importantly, having access to consumer deposits is lowering LendingClub's cost of capital (versus having to draw more costly institutional short-term funding).

As shown in the chart below, the company's average cost on its liabilities fell -5bps sequentially and -58bps y/y to 3.75%. Meanwhile, net interest margin improved 56bps y/y to 5.98%, which helped to drive 14% y/y growth in net interest income to $163.0 million.

Though double-digit originations growth is LendingClub's headline metric, we find it important as well to keep tracking the success and growth of the company's depositor base, which is continuing to lower LendingClub's cost of capital. Further growth catalysts are also ahead, including the company's push into home improvement financing in 2026 and opening up a new loan origination type.

Key takeaways

Historically, many investors have approached LendingClub with skepticism. And yet it's very evident from recent results that the core business is in excellent shape. LendingClub is seeing heightened demand from borrowers and loan investors, which is setting up a strong flywheel for originations growth, while the recently-released checking and savings accounts are giving LendingClub access to low-cost deposits like its banking peers. With the stock trading at ~11x P/E, LendingClub not only trades below the S&P 500, but even below many large-cap banks in the mid-teens (a group that has historically been discounted).

Stay long here and buy with confidence post-earnings.

data.title: Telefonica: I'm Surprised At The 2026-2030 Plan
data.description:
Telefonica (TEF) remains a relatively undercovered telecommunications stock on SA. A good reflection of this is that the last few articles on the company, with the latest one found here, are predominantly my own. This is interesting from several points of view and perspectives. First of all, because the company is so significant in size, scope and exposure. Secondly, because my coverage on the company has been mostly "correct" for the past year or so. By correct, I mean that I accurately rated both "BUY" and "HOLD" when it was time to exit. I also exited at the correct time, with my latest exit of the final remnants of the position being in August of last year. This was prior both to a significant FX underperformance, but more importantly, a significant share price underperformance as well.

If you see above, you can note what has been happening since my coverage. What I'll be doing in this article is to determine whether the drop means that a "BUY" is now the best choice again.

A few things to help us in this determination. First of all, we have the company's CMD which was in November, where it presented a brand new 2026-2030 strategy. I will be looking at this. Secondly, updated valuation and updated market outlook for telecommunications companies as a whole.

I'm personally mostly "out" of telcos. Most of the ones, especially European ones, have become significantly overvalued. This was a major shift - at one time, almost 20% of my portfolio was entirely devoted to telcos, enjoying their good yield and stable earnings. But when a company reaches overvaluation, it doesn't really matter how good its fundamentals are, to me. There's always elsewhere that's good to invest, and that carries less valuation-related risk.

With that out of the way, let's see what we have here.

Telefonica - Upside in a few contexts following the significant drop

Let's first begin by determining what caused the company to decline so significantly in what is a relatively short period of time - 5-6 months. Usually telcos don't move in that fashion, unless there's a clear reason for it. For this, the clear reason is the announcement of a significant dividend.

The 2026 dividend was slashed in half. This was, to be clear, a surprise to me. I did not expect, nor forecast a dividend cut. In fact, I considered it to be a "safe" dividend at the time of my last article coverage. This decision of the dividend cut was part of the company's new strategic approach - more on that approach later. To be clear, the company's dividend was well-covered by earnings and cash flow - but the combination of reduced FCF forecasts, and Telefonica being by far the riskiest of European telcos leads me to say that out of all, if one was to do it, it was this one (with Vodafone (VOD) having already cut it some time ago).

It was perhaps also related to the volatility and performance uncertainty in its LATAM segments, including the markets of Peru, Uruguay, Ecuador and Colombia. These markets are obviously adding political and FX risks to "the pot". However, I view these emerging markets as a potential growth driver, which legacy markets in Europe certainly is not - so the decision to leave these, even some of them, is something I view with a healthy dose of scepticism.

The new strategic plan is concerned with lowering costs. AI is part of it, but not the entire story. Part of the decline is likely also a reaction to this by the company, which is cutting over 5,000 jobs in Spain alone. Investments will be in infrastructure upgrades and AI.

The company's market outlook works with a legacy expectation of consolidation, demands and higher investments in tech. Telefonica expects AI to "transform" processes and network, an expectation I view as unlikely. The notion that legacy systems would be upgraded to cater AI is not one I see as likely, especially when the company in question is already leveraged very highly. Furthermore, the way that telco's store and work with data is not something that's easy for AI to work with. Telco data is stored in so-called OSS and BSS systems - accessing these for real-time data and decision making for genAI or any AI is next to impossible because of inherent security requirements and fragmented infrastructure in addition to heavy fragmentation. Note that these are also legal requirements under EU law (GDPR) - these will not be changing, if anything, they will be improving.

Take these statements as one of the reasons why I am hesitant and dubious about the company's plans.

I think the above-mentioned "strategic pillars" make some sense, for the most part, but I doubt very much the company will be able to see the savings and profits that it expects from its AI investments - the costs saved by headcount reduction will likely go straight back into AI infrastructure and process support. This assumption, that headcount savings go straight into capital and operational requirements of AI (not to mention hidden labor costs and various execution-related challenges), is proven, with productivity and savings promises not being realized.

Put it simply, or even if you dilute it somewhat, this is a major risk that Telefonica is taking. Combine this with the company's growth targets for the coming years.

Furthermore, Telefonica's own assumptions and argumentations are inherently flawed.

The reason that the European telco market is fragmented, and absolutely likely to remain so compared to US/China is that US/China are countries - each one with the same language. Europe consists of five languages each with more than 50 million speakers each, 24 official languages in 28 countries in the EU alone. The notion that this is subject to consolidation is, to me, laughable. If you spend even the slightest time looking at the various conflicts and discussions in the EU to execute relatively simple things (And the EU has been at it for almost 80+ years), as well as how tricky it is for most multinational European telcos to manage more than one "home market" - let's just say that as a professional analyst, I was very surprised that this is where the company is going/wants to go.

To state that I don't believe in the plan, would be putting it lightly. Even with new regulatory frameworks, telco scale in Europe and the effects it would have, is to me unlikely. The fact that part of its plan is to exit its HispAm growth segment in favor of European legacy, where it holds very little advantage is, again, surprising.

Both Germany and the UK are incredibly difficult markets, where several major players already operate successfully. The notion that Germany has "untapped opportunity in fiber", seems to completely disregard fundamental growth challenges that I often cite in my articles on Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY)

The notion further, that the most-leveraged telco in Europe would somehow make something of either of these markets is not something that I can agree with. Telefonica has been doing good in what it already has in these markets. This doesn't mean its likely to massively improve.

The cited growth levers for the company are unlikely to me. I don't see catalysts in Europe, a very mature market, for short-term growth or rebound that would justify a higher valuation under these premises for Telefonica. In fact, with a slashed dividend, I believe most European telcos just became more attractive than this one.

I view the company's cost-cutting as unrealistic given the complexity of its growth plans. Cited headcount reduction will hardly result in EBITA/EBITDAaL increase without revenue growth, and given its asset sales and other plans, revenue growth will be hard to come by in mostly mature markets. It's forecasted focus on the so-called "Telefonica Tech" (which is supposed to be about 50% of the revenue growth) seems wildly positive to me. Given every company's focus on so-called cybersecurity and cloud tech segments, this is now a commoditized service, very unlikely to command significant premia.

I am surprised at this move - but as any objective and valuation-based analyst, I react to the company's new forecasts with my own objective forecast.

Valuation for Telefonica - I'm cutting my targets

I am very pleased that I rotated my position in the company. Given what management has done, I view it as a good choice on my part to set very conservative targets to exit this investment at the right price. I would not want to be in it now, unless I got my shares very cheap.

I am disappointed in the company's new plan, which I view as unrealistic, and my forecasts will reflect this. My last PT for the company was €4.3/share. This would have technically put the company at a "BUY" here - but I'm lowering it. Current native share price is €3.35/share. The new yield of €0.15/share means the yield is now below 4.5%/share. It also means that at 8-9x P/E, the company does not offer a market-beating upside, despite the significant loss/downturn for the company.

Those of you looking at the forecasts and believing it to be a good contrarian investment, remember that Telefonica negatively misses estimates by over 80% of the time the past 10 years, even with a 20% margin of error. It's not a precise nor "easy" company. With its previous growth segments intact, I believed the volatility was made up for by the valuation and upside. Now I see high costs (that will eat away at asset sale capital), without much low-risk upside in legacy markets. I believe the forecasts to be too exuberant. I forecast only €0.3/share for 2026E, and €0.32/share for 2027E - and the 2028E will, in my view, remain below €0.36/share.

Telefonica has the lowest IG-rating in all European telcos, a BBB-. In order for me to consider it appealing, I would want market-beating RoR from the lowest 20-year market valuation of about 8-10x P/E, which brings the market value to €4/share. That means I put my PT at €2.9/share, at which point we'd be close to a 5% yield.

At that price I would be willing to take the risk, but not here.

This is the biggest cut in PT I've done in a very long time. The company's new plan, 3Q25 results, and 2028-2030E forecast came as a surprise. I've been waiting with this article a while because I wanted to see what the market did - and I haven't been disappointed. I believe the market reaction to the company's ambitions perfectly reflects my own sentiment - Telefonica is now worth less money.

My new PT is €2.9/share, to an FV of €4/share. If you're looking for safe alternatives in telco, try either Verizon (VZ) for the US market, or VOD for the EU one - though Vodafone is not exactly cheap any longer either.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & considerations for the company

I believe the CMD with the new targets presentation and the dividend cut marks a definite change in the company. The reason I did not update the article until now was also because the company had the "right" rating from me - "HOLD". Execution risks for the company's new plan are so significant and the growth targets so unrealistic given what's involved, that combined with growth market divestments, I view the company as the least investable in Europe.

That is, unless the company was trading at a significant discount. To me that significant discount is now below €3/share.

The main risks have been mentioned above - one that I will add as a tech-specific one is that Telefonica lacks meaningful differentiation in its revenue-growth lauded Telefonica tech segment. Its role seems, based on presentations and comments, to be one of an integrator, rather than creating any sort of value or exclusive tech for its customers. The notion that 50% of revenue growth will come from integrating and reselling existing, proprietary products and services from others is downright unrealistic to me.

This, combined with the company's targets of lowering its CapEx to below 12% of sales, would mean that it's also the least investing telco out there. The cited plan is the risk here - I view it as unrealistic.

Thesis

Telefonica was once cheap, but it is no longer cheap for what it offers as of its update in 2025-2026. I no longer have any position, nor do I intend to buy or get one, unless the company gets substantially cheaper even than now. I view this one as a "Hold" with a $3.3/share price target for the ADR. This marks a severe and serious cut in company targets and pricing, but given what Telefónica's new plans are, I view it as justified.

The November-presented CMD changes the entire thesis for the company, and what I expect from it. My new native PT is €2.9/share, and even at that price, I would be careful buying here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, a price target of 2.9/share is where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: Artisan Global Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Artisan Global Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Performance Discussion

The portfolio generated a negative return during Q4, trailing the MSCI AC World Index. From a relative standpoint, the quarter was weaker than expected, particularly given that many of the portfolio’s largest positions reported solid operating performance. However, portfolio performance was driven primarily by idiosyncratic drivers, particularly among a few of our larger holdings, where short-term share price weakness outweighed otherwise supportive fundamentals.

Five of the portfolio’s top ten holdings declined during an upmarket, which is uncharacteristic of our process and results. Several higher conviction positions that had performed well earlier in the year paused during the quarter, which had an outsized impact given their larger weights (Tencent (TCEHY), BAE Systems, Baker Hughes (BKR)). Notably, as of this writing, several of these names have subsequently rebounded in early January, retracing much of the quarter’s decline. Additionally, Oracle and Netflix were the main detractors during the period following strong performance runs YTD.

On the positive side, our overweight in health care, which was the top-performing sector in the index for the quarter, contributed to returns. Several health care franchises delivered strong results as product launches gained momentum and investor sentiment toward the sector improved.

For the full year, the portfolio generated a strong absolute return but lagged the MSCI AC World Index. For much of the year, index performance was heavily influenced by a small number of very large constituents, creating a challenging backdrop for active portfolios. The underperformance of mid-cap growth stocks amid continued mega-cap leadership also weighed on results. While several portfolio holdings delivered strong fundamental progress over the course of the year, relative results were shaped by index concentration effects and short-term reversals in some larger positions during Q4.

Among our top contributors in Q4 were Argenx, Lam Research and Insmed. Argenx (ARGX) is a commercial-stage biotechnology company with a first-in-class autoimmune therapy, VYVGART®, approved for myasthenia gravis and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. In its latest earnings report, VYVGART® sales nearly doubled YoY, rising 96% and bringing annualized sales to over $4 billion, supported by increased new patient uptake following the launch of its prefilled syringe formulation. Late in the quarter, the stock pulled back after the company discontinued a midstage trial to treat thyroid eye disease due to a lack of efficacy. While this took away some of the upside for the quarter, we continue to view additional indications for VYVGART® as a compelling opportunity, with the drug currently being studied to treat more than a dozen other diseases.

Lam (LRCX) is a leading supplier of semiconductor manufacturing equipment, with dominant positions in etching and deposition, two of the three critical steps in chip fabrication. Lam delivered better-than-expected Q3 revenue and margins, with particularly strong demand from China. Management expects its geographic mix to normalize next year due to other regions accelerating. Q4 guidance was also ahead of expectations, supported by customers pulling forward demand tied to future growth areas, despite ongoing export restrictions in certain parts of the market. More broadly, demand for memory chips is incredibly strong, and Lam is uniquely positioned to facilitate future capacity additions.

Insmed (INSM) is a biotechnology company focused on pulmonary diseases. Its lead product, ARIKAYCE®, continues to perform well, with management raising full-year revenue guidance following 22% YoY growth. The company also reported a strong launch for Brinsupri™ (brensocatib), the first approved therapy for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis, generating $28 million in initial sales with more than 2,500 patients and 1,700 prescribers. We remain encouraged by Insmed’s execution and believe Brinsupri™ is likely to drive a substantial profit cycle in the coming quarters and years.

Among our biggest detractors in Q4 were Oracle, Netflix and BAE Systems. Oracle (ORCL) is a leading global provider of enterprise software, cloud solutions and database technologies. We believe the company will be a leader in cloud infrastructure, a newer business for it that is increasingly being deployed to enable AI use cases, while also benefiting its core software and database businesses. Recent earnings were softer than expected, with investors focused on elevated capital spending requirements and limited near-term revenue upside, despite strong growth in Oracle Cloud Infrastructure and a sizable increase in backlog. The stock pulled back sharply in the quarter as investors reassessed the pace and capital intensity of AI-infrastructure spending and grew more cautious on near-term growth expectations, following several months of elevated enthusiasm. Notably, Oracle is positioned to be a prime beneficiary of Open AI’s future growth, given its role as a key cloud infrastructure partner supporting model training and deployment. During the quarter, however, investor sentiment was impacted as other model builders (Google Gemini and Anthropic’s Claude) temporarily leapfrogged OpenAI’s ChatGPT, contributing to some short-term skepticism, which we view as temporary. We trimmed the position earlier in the year as valuation expanded and capital requirements increased. Following the recent pullback, we believe the stock more appropriately reflects these trade-offs and are monitoring execution and funding decisions closely as the company balances ambition with financial discipline.

Netflix (NFLX) shares declined in Q4 despite a strong earnings report, as results fell short of investors’ elevated expectations. Recent M&A activity in the media landscape has added uncertainty around strategy, execution and regulation—especially with Netflix exploring Warner Bros Discovery assets. This move signals a shift from Netflix’s historical organic growth approach. While we remain confident in Netflix’s long-term strengths in content and monetization, these changes led us to further reduce our position to manage risk, given the potential for a significantly different business profile after the deal.

BAE Systems (BAESY) is Europe’s largest defense contractor and a key supplier to the US Department of Defense. We believe the company is well positioned for a multiyear defense spending cycle, driven by higher NATO targets and US re-arming efforts. These trends are likely to increase procurement of advanced weapons, fueling strong demand for missiles, drones and space systems. Backed by a sizable backlog and improving margins, we believe BAE offers solid growth potential. We also expect defense spending to remain resilient and anticipate order conversions to accelerate in 2026–2027. Shares paused this quarter after a strong year, and we added to our position on the pullback.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated new positions in RTX (Raytheon), Capital One and Somnigroup. RTX Corp (RTX) is a global aerospace and defense company with operations across aircraft systems, engines and defense technologies. Within defense, Raytheon is the leading manufacturer of missile defense systems such as the Patriot, with years of backlog and visibility supported by global re-arming priorities. We expect orders and backlog to continue growing as global defense spending rises and commercial aerospace demand remains steady. We also anticipate margin improvement in the engine business as it progresses beyond the peak of its fleet inspection and remediation cycle, supporting better efficiency and profitability.

Capital One (COF) is one of the largest US consumer lenders and among the best underwriters of revolving credit globally. As one of the original fintechs, we believe it will benefit from leveraging technology and AI to drive efficiency. We also expect the recent Discover acquisition to be transformative, expanding Capital One’s debit economics and creating an opportunity to leverage Discover’s payment rails as a competitive alternative to Mastercard. Supported by strong underwriting, a healthy consumer backdrop and potential tailwinds from US tax refunds, we initiated a Garden SM position.

Somnigroup (SGI) is the world’s largest bedding company with in-house manufacturing and leading brands including Tempur-Pedic®, Sealy® and Stearns & Foster®. Its recent acquisition of Mattress Firm has increased scale, accelerated market share gains and driven margin expansion through pricing power, cost synergies and deeper vertical integration. It is also pursuing Leggett & Platt (LEG), a potential acquisition that would further strengthen proprietary design and technology capabilities. The bedding industry is at cyclical lows in part due to its ties to housing. However, early signs of a stabilizing housing market and furniture spending suggest upside potential for household formation and a normalization in furniture spending. With strong brands, growing share and leverage to an eventual recovery in housing and lower interest rates, we believe Somnigroup is well positioned for long-term growth.

In addition to Insmed and BAE, we also added to L3Harris (LHX), a global aerospace and defense company. We believe it is well positioned to benefit from growing investment in next-generation missile defense and national security programs that require advanced propulsion, satellite systems and low-Earth orbit tracking capabilities. These efforts represent a large and expanding market opportunity that could support faster revenue growth over the coming years. In addition, as warfare increasingly involves low-cost drone threats, L3Harris’s strengths in sensing and counter-drone technologies position it well to address a critical and evolving defense need. We added to our position during the quarter at what we believe to be an opportunistic entry point following recent share price weakness.

In Q4, we exited several Garden SM campaigns after determining that, while the underlying businesses remain high quality, our investment theses were not progressing as expected. These exits included PayPal (PYPL), Coinbase (COIN), Zoetis (ZTS), L’Oréal (LRLCY), Starbucks (SBUX) and Sage Group (SGGEF). This reflects our disciplined approach to move on rather than remain early in ideas that were not gaining traction. Typically, we exit three or four stocks per quarter, but a larger-than-usual number of Garden SM holdings required a more decisive cleanup in Q4. We redeployed the capital into established Crop SM holdings that had pulled back after strong earlier performance, where we saw favorable timing. We have a robust pipeline of new ideas, as reflected in our recent Garden SM investments.

Along with Netflix, other notable trims during the quarter were Snowflake and Boston Scientific. Snowflake (SNOW) is a leading player in data infrastructure, enabling organizations to unlock greater value from their data estates. We trimmed the position following a strong run, given the elevated valuation and the potential for revenue growth to decelerate amid uneven consumption trends. Furthermore, Snowflake is most commonly used by customers for structured data; the potential for large language models to help enterprises effectively unlock the benefits of unstructured data further questions Snowflake's value proposition. This is a question we continue to monitor and learn about.

Boston Scientific (BSX), a global leader in minimally invasive medical devices, has been in our portfolio since 2015. Over that time, the company has executed well by investing in R&D and acquiring assets in high-growth areas such as structural heart, urology and minimally invasive surgery, which have driven diversification, revenue growth and stronger cash flow. As the investment campaign has matured and growth has slowed, sustaining the high growth rates that once supported a larger position has become more difficult. While we continue to view the business as high quality, we reduced the position during the quarter.

Carefully consider the Fund’s investment objective, risks and charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in the Fund's prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by calling 800.344.1770. Read carefully before investing.

data.title: Amphenol: A Tough Proposition Into 2026
data.description:
In October, I concluded that I was impressed and concerned at the same time in the case of Amphenol (APH), which employed a very aggressive M&A strategy, including acquisitions from CommScope, now rebranded as Vistance Networks (VISN), and Trexon.

That, however, discounted the impressive organic growth achievements of Amphenol, which have simply been very impressive. The company ended 2025 on a solid note, yet guides either softly or conservatively for the first quarter.

This, combined with ever-increasing valuations, creates a tough setup this year, even after the plunge following the earnings release.

An Electronic Revolution

Amphenol aims to facilitate the electronics revolution by providing a wide range of high-technology interconnect, sensor, and antenna solutions. The company is a massive player, generating about $25 billion in sales from providing such products while employing over 150,000 workers who produce these products. These include connectors, antennas, sensors, flex, cables, and others for harsh environments, which require high speed, lots of power, and others.

The company is very well diversified, with North America and Asia both making up about 40% of sales, complemented by smaller European activities. End market diversification is solid, although the IT-datacom segment is outsized, making up a third of sales.

The company aims to outperform the market by quick reactivity, customer focus, agile business performance, and strong and flexible capital allocation decisions. The business was founded in 1932, went public in 1957, and went public again in 1991, as strong achievements were realised recently. This is in part driven by M&A, with some 50 businesses acquired over the past decade.

Gauging Expectations

Traditionally, Amphenol has been a solid grower, with revenues tripling in the decade leading up to 2024, as revenues were reported up 21% to $15.3 billion that year, driven by a 13% increase in organic sales. Adjusted earnings came in just shy of $2 per share, as a $70 valuation was already demanding a mid-thirty times earnings multiple.

What followed was an unparalleled period of momentum, however, driven by AI, as first-quarter sales rose by no less than 48% to $4.8 billion, in its turn driven by a 33% increase in organic sales. Organic sales growth accelerated to 41% in the second quarter. Over the summer, the company reached a second deal with the former CommScope business to acquire its connectivity & cable solutions business in a $10.5 billion deal, adding $3.6 billion in sales.

Pro forma net debt was seen around $15 billion, a number seen increasing a bit as the company announced another $1 billion deal to acquire Trexon, adding further interconnected and cable assembly activities to the lineup. Third-quarter organic sales grew by another 41%. Not fearful about the added debt, the company announced a 52% increase in the dividend, payable at a dollar per share.

Trading at $128 in October, equity was valued at $163 billion, for a $180 billion pro forma enterprise valuation. This was applied to a business generating about $25 billion in sales as earnings were trending at around $4 per share, for a mid-thirty times earnings multiple.

It was the fourth-quarter guidance that made me have reservations, with fourth-quarter sales seen between $6.0 and $6.1 billion, suggesting minimal sequential sales declines. Having become a real AI proxy, I feared the lack of sequential improvements, as any concerns about the longevity of the AI boom and demand could leave shares vulnerable to a setback.

Setback Arrives, Albeit at High Levels

Since October, shares of Amphenol have seen continued gains as shares rose to a high of $167 in recent trading action, gaining another $40 in just about a quarter of the time. Currently, shares trade 13% lower in response to the fourth quarter results (or actually the Q1 outlook), although they are still trading at $144 per share.

Earlier this month, the company closed on the acquisition of the CommScope assets, furthermore commenting that the segment was expected to contribute $4.1 billion in sales to the 2026 results while being accretive to the tune of $0.15 per share.

The share price setback was seen following the fourth quarter results being released. Fourth quarter sales of $6.4 billion blasted by the original guidance, with reported sales up 49% and organic sales up 37%. For the year, revenues were up 52% to $23.1 billion as the adjusted earnings rose by 77% to $3.34 per share, as the fourth-quarter earnings number of $0.97 per share more or less confirmed the $4 earnings per share run rate.

The problem is the outlook for the first quarter, with revenues seen between $6.9 and $7.0 billion, including a $900 million revenue contribution from the acquisition of the CommScope assets, suggesting real sequential revenue declines. Adjusted earnings were seen down to $0.92 per share.

Net debt was reported at $4.1 billion as of the fourth quarter, for a pro forma net debt load of $14.6 billion. Some deleveraging has been achieved already, in part as the company is slowly diluting the shareholder base at current levels, which makes sense.

Caution

Frankly, I am quite cautious. While the company has a habit of being a bit cautious, the first quarter outlook is soft, even if the company ends up delivering a big beat. In fact, earnings power around $4 per share is actually seen in a $3.50-4.00 per share range, while the stock has, on a net basis, gained ground since October (despite the current setback).

In fact, shares are now back to the level seen less than two weeks ago, as a testament to the momentum already enjoyed and the pace thereof.

The guidance is soft as the implied earnings multiple has moved up to the high thirties, which makes me cautious despite the great capital allocation track record and positioning. It feels that the risk-reward seems to be skewed to the negative side here amidst elevated expectations into 2026.

data.title: Artisan Global Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Process

We seek to invest in companies that possess franchise characteristics, are benefiting from an accelerating profit cycle and are trading at a discount to our estimate of private market value. Our investment process focuses on two distinct elements—security selection and capital allocation. We overlay our investment process with broad knowledge of the global economy.

Security Selection

We seek to identify companies that have franchise characteristics (e.g., low-cost production capability, possession of a proprietary asset, dominant market share or a defensible brand name), are benefiting from an accelerating profit cycle and are trading at a discount to our estimate of private market value. We also assess governance and other material sustainability factors that could impact future stock returns. We look for companies that are well positioned for long-term growth, which is driven by demand for their products and services, at an early enough stage in their profit cycle to benefit from the increased cash flows produced by the emerging profit cycle.

Capital Allocation

Based on our fundamental analysis of a company’s profit cycle, we divide the portfolio into three parts. Garden SM investments are small positions in the early part of their profit cycle that may warrant more sizeable allocations as their profit cycle accelerates. Crop SM investments are positions that are being increased to a full weight because they are moving through the strongest part of their profit cycles. Harvest SM investments are positions that are being reduced as they near our estimates of full valuation or their profit cycles begin to decelerate.

Broad Knowledge

We overlay the security selection and capital allocation elements of our investment process with a desire to invest opportunistically across the entire global economy. We seek broad knowledge of the global economy in order to find growth wherever it occurs.

Team Overview

We believe deep industry expertise, broad investment knowledge, a highly collaborative decision-making process and individual accountability are a powerful combination. Since the inception of the team, we have been committed to building a team of growth investors that retains these attributes and is solely dedicated to our process and approach.

Investing Environment

Global equities posted another solid gain during Q4, capping off a record year. Despite strong headline performance, the path through 2025 was marked by elevated volatility and frequent shifts in sentiment. Investors navigated a complex backdrop shaped by political developments in the US, evolving trade policy rhetoric, changing expectations for monetary policy and ongoing geopolitical tensions.

Markets experienced periods of macro uncertainty in 2025 as concerns emerged around slowing US growth, signs of consumer fatigue and persistent inflation. Trade policy developments were a recurring source of volatility, including the announcement of new tariffs on steel, aluminum and autos, as well as speculation around the scope and timing of additional measures. These dynamics contributed to periodic risk-off moves, even as markets ultimately marched higher.

Rate cuts by global central banks helped to buoy equity markets throughout the year. The Federal Reserve cut rates a total of three times in 2025. The most recent December cut included increased internal division and language signaling a higher bar for future cuts. Markets nonetheless interpreted the December decision and Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s tone as incrementally dovish. Outside the US, central banks in several other regions, including the euro zone, UK, Canada and Australia, cut rates in 2025. Overall, intermittent signs of softer labor data and consumer-related concerns were largely offset by rate cuts, resilient economic activity and a stronger-than-expected late-year US GDP print.

Equity gains were also supported by strong corporate earnings and the continued investment boom in artificial intelligence (AI). However, technology stocks pulled back late in the year as investors reassessed the pace and scale of AI-related capital expenditures. This reassessment led to relative weakness in the sector, as markets rotated toward health care and more cyclical areas.

There were other notable leadership shifts during 2025, with returns broadening across geographies and styles. In contrast to recent years, international equities outperformed US markets, and emerging markets delivered their strongest annual return since 2017, aided by a weaker dollar, improving earnings momentum and earlier policy easing relative to developed markets. Large-cap stocks outperformed small caps for both the quarter and the year, while mid-cap stocks lagged, with mid cap growth the worst performing segment of the investing universe across both time frames. Further down the cap spectrum, microcap outperformed all cap tiers. In contrast to the rest of 2025, value outperformed growth during Q4.
